items.title: Question before library
items.avoid: Opening Seaborn or Plotly docs and picking the first example chart that runs.
items.prefer: Write one sentence: "I need to compare ___ across ___" or "I need the distribution of ___." Then map to bar, line, scatter, hist, box, or heatmap.
items.why: Library tutorials optimize for API demos, not your dataset's analytical goal.
items.example: "Compare median latency across five models" → box plot, then sns.boxplot or px.box.

items.title: One message per panel
items.avoid: Cramming class balance, correlation matrix, and loss curve into one axes without subplots.
items.prefer: Separate figures or subplots, each with a chart family matched to one question.
items.why: Mixed encodings overload readers in notebooks and make slides unusable.
items.example: EDA notebook: one hist per feature grid, one heatmap page, one line panel for training metrics.

items.title: Match chart to variable scale
items.avoid: Bar charts of raw counts when proportions are the fair comparison across unequal totals.
items.prefer: State whether counts or normalized proportions answer the question; label the y-axis accordingly.
items.why: Mislabeled axes cause wrong conclusions about imbalance or market share in segmented ML metrics.
items.example: Segment sizes differ: bar of within-segment error rate, not raw error count, for fair comparison.

items.title: Follow heatmap with targeted scatter
items.avoid: Stopping EDA after a correlation heatmap when nonlinear relationships drive model choice.
items.prefer: Heatmap for screening; scatter on flagged pairs before feature engineering or polynomial terms.
items.why: Pearson correlation is linear; ML models may need transforms visible only in scatter.
items.example: Heatmap |r|>0.8 between sqft and price → scatter reveals diminishing returns curve.
